title: Rule of thirds
body: Divide the frame into a three-by-three grid and place key elements, a horizon, a subject's eyes, along those lines or at their intersections rather than dead center. Off-center placement tends to feel more dynamic and gives the eye somewhere to travel, while dead-center placement reads as static, which is exactly why it's the right choice when static and confrontational is the intended effect. Most cameras can overlay this grid directly in the viewfinder or on the live view screen.

title: Leading lines
body: A road, a railing, a shoreline, any line in the frame naturally pulls the eye along its length. Leading lines use that reflex to guide the viewer toward the subject, often from a foreground corner toward a point deeper in the frame, which also builds a sense of depth. Converging lines (railway tracks toward a vanishing point) are the strongest version of this, but even a subtle diagonal shadow or fence line does the same job at a smaller scale.

title: Framing
body: A frame within the frame, a doorway, a window, an archway, overhanging branches, isolates the subject and adds depth by giving the image a clear foreground layer. It also does double duty as context: shooting a subject through a market stall's awning tells the viewer where they are without a caption. The technique works with literal architectural frames and with looser ones, a crowd's shoulders parting around a central figure.

title: Symmetry and pattern
body: A repeating pattern, tiles, windows, a crowd in matching uniforms, creates visual rhythm, and a break in that pattern, one different tile, one person facing the other way, becomes instantly the most interesting thing in the frame because it violates the expectation the pattern just built. Perfect symmetry (a reflection in still water, a centered architectural facade) works similarly: it reads as calm and ordered, which makes it a strong choice when the subject itself is calm or formal, and a strange one when it isn't.

title: Negative space
body: The empty area around a subject, a plain sky, a blank wall, is not wasted frame, it's what gives the subject room to breathe and lets scale and isolation read clearly. A small figure against a large empty field communicates smallness or solitude precisely because there's nothing else competing for attention. Negative space is also where captions, ad copy, or a later crop can live without ever touching the subject.

title: Depth and layering
body: A photograph is flat, but a foreground, midground, and background, even a loosely composed one, gives the eye a path through the image and a sense of three-dimensional space. Placing something in the immediate foreground, out of focus or not, an overhanging branch, a blurred hand, pushes the main subject back into its own distinct layer and makes the whole scene feel more spatial than a single flat plane would.

title: Color and contrast
body: Complementary colors (blue and orange, red and green) sitting opposite each other on the color wheel create visual tension and make a subject pop against its background, which is why it's the most common color grade in film and advertising. Contrast in tone works the same way structurally: a dark subject against a bright background, or the reverse, separates figure from ground even before color or focus does any of the work.

title: Balance
body: An off-center subject needs something, however small, on the opposite side of the frame to keep the composition from feeling like it's toppling over, a smaller secondary object, a patch of color, even just open space of a different tone. This isn't the same as symmetry: balance can be entirely asymmetrical, a large subject on one side counterweighted by a small but visually heavy detail on the other, as long as the two sides feel like they're in conversation rather than one being simply empty.

title: Golden ratio and Fibonacci spiral
body: A refinement of the rule of thirds: instead of an even grid, the frame is divided by a ratio (roughly 1 to 1.618) found throughout natural growth patterns, shells, flowers, spiral galaxies. Plotted as a spiral, it suggests a path for the eye that curves inward toward the subject rather than snapping to a straight grid line. It's subtler and harder to apply deliberately than the rule of thirds, which is why most photographers use it as a check after the fact rather than a framing tool in the moment.

title: Rule of space
body: A subject that is moving, looking, or gesturing in a direction needs room in the frame to move, look, or gesture into. Crop that space away and the image feels cramped and unresolved, as if the subject is about to walk straight into the edge of the photograph. Leaving that room, sometimes called nose room or lead room, is a small adjustment that makes a directional subject feel like it belongs inside the frame rather than trapped at its border.

title: Perspective and vantage point
body: Where the camera is placed relative to the subject changes what the image means, not just what it shows. A low angle looking up makes a subject read as powerful or looming, the classic hero shot. A high angle looking down can make a subject read as small or vulnerable. Eye level reads as neutral and direct. Extreme versions, a bird's eye view flattening a crowd into pattern, a worm's eye view from ground level, trade familiarity for drama, and are usually chosen precisely because they force the viewer to see something ordinary in an unfamiliar way.

title: Juxtaposition
body: Placing two unlike things in the same frame, old beside new, wealth beside poverty, a static object beside implied motion, makes a viewer compare them whether the image says anything about it directly or not. This is a compositional decision as much as a conceptual one: it depends on physically arranging or waiting for both elements to occupy the frame together, at a scale and position where the contrast reads immediately rather than needing a caption to explain it.

title: Fill the frame and minimalism
body: The opposite move from negative space: cropping in tight enough that the subject, or a single detail of it, occupies almost the entire frame, removing context and surroundings entirely. This forces attention onto texture, shape, and gesture rather than place, and is common in macro work, in portraiture that wants total intimacy, and in minimalist compositions that reduce a scene to one shape, one line, one gesture, and nothing else competing for the eye.

title: Rhythm and repetition
body: A series of similar elements, columns, waves, a row of streetlights, spaced across the frame creates a visual beat the eye follows across the image, similar to symmetry and pattern but looser: the repetition doesn't need to be perfectly regular to work, and a gradual change across the sequence, receding size, shifting color, adds a sense of movement or time passing that a perfectly uniform pattern doesn't.
